front.title: Recommendations for AI-Driven Network Operations Work in the IETF

abstract.t: A growing number of individual Internet-Drafts address the use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) in network operations and management. These efforts are currently spread across multiple working groups and mailing lists without a coordinated standards-track path. This document surveys the current landscape, identifies what the IETF is well-positioned to standardize, and proposes a concrete set of deliverables: YANG data models for AI agent audit logs, configuration, governance, and operational state; a NETCONF/RESTCONF attribution mechanism for AI-initiated changes; and an operational security BCP for AI-mediated network management.

note.name: Discussion Venues
note.t: Discussion of this document should take place on the OPSAWG mailing list (opsawg@ietf.org) or the AINETOPS mailing list (ainetops@ietf.org).

section.title: Introduction
section.t: AI and ML techniques are increasingly being applied to network operations: anomaly detection, closed-loop remediation, intent translation, and autonomous device management. The IETF community has responded with a growing number of individual drafts exploring governance frameworks , network management agent architectures , use cases , and research challenges .

However, these efforts remain disconnected. There is no chartered working group, no agreed set of deliverables, and no clear standards-track path for the work. Without coordination, the community risks producing overlapping informational documents rather than interoperable standards.

This document identifies where the IETF can make a unique and concrete contribution — specifically through YANG data models, protocol extensions, and operational guidance — and proposes a focused set of deliverables toward that end. It also identifies areas that are better left to research, industry forums, or other bodies.

section.title: Conventions and Definitions
section.t: The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here.

section.title: Current Landscape
section.t: The following efforts are relevant to AI-driven network operations in the IETF and IRTF:

ul.li: proposes a governance framework of thirteen principles for AI-mediated autonomous network device management, covering human authority, safety, auditability, reversibility, and bounded autonomy.

defines a Network Management Agent (NMA) concept, an AI- driven entity that bridges service intent and network operations using an Intent-Awareness-Analysis-Decision- Execution (IAADE) control loop. The draft identifies three interfaces — Agent-to-User (A2U), Agent-to-Controller (A2C), and Agent-to-Network (A2N) — but explicitly defers the definition of the A2C interface to future documents.
documents use cases including reactive troubleshooting, proactive assurance, closed-loop optimization, misconfiguration detection, and virtual operator assistance.
identifies research challenges for coupling AI with network management, including distribution of AI tasks and privacy concerns.
The AINETOPS non-WG mailing list (ainetops@ietf.org) was established to discuss integration of AI and ML in network operations using IETF technologies.

section.t: Collectively, these efforts have established the problem space and use cases. The next step is to translate this into standards-track work with concrete, interoperable deliverables.

section.title: Where IETF Can Contribute
section.t: The IETF's established strengths lie in defining YANG data models, protocol behavior, and operational guidelines. Applied to AI-driven network operations, this means:

ul.li: Standardizing the data models that describe how AI agents are configured, governed, and monitored — so that operators can manage AI agents through the same NETCONF/RESTCONF interfaces they already use for network devices.
Extending existing management protocols to carry attribution metadata for AI-initiated changes, enabling interoperable audit trails and rollback.
Producing operational security guidance as a BCP, so that operators deploying AI agents can follow a common baseline regardless of vendor implementation.

section.t: The IETF is not well-positioned to specify AI algorithms, model architectures, or training methodologies. Those topics belong in the IRTF or in industry forums.

section.title: Working Group Scope
section.t: The AINETOPS mailing list work should be chartered into OPSAWG or it itsef should be chartered as a working group with a tight, deliverable-focused scope. The charter should commit to the work items described in this section and explicitly exclude architecture surveys, gap analyses, and AI algorithm specification.

A use cases and requirements document, building on , should be the first adopted work item. This document will not be published as an RFC but will inform the scope and priorities of the YANG data model work.

section.title: YANG Data Models
section.t: Four YANG data models are proposed, each addressing a distinct aspect of AI agent management.

section.title: Intent Submission YANG Model
section.t: specifies that operators and peer agents submit intents to the NMA via the Agent-to-User (A2U) interface using structured formats (YANG, JSON, or gNMI). The intent carries a high-level operational goal (e.g., "restore SLA for service X" or "optimize traffic across domain Y") along with associated constraints such as latency bounds, cost limits, and affected resource scope.

IETF should standardize this intent submission format as a YANG data model, covering: the intent itself, the constraints attached to it, the submitting entity (operator or peer agent), the target scope, and the status of intent fulfillment. This model provides the northbound interface through which human operators and orchestration systems interact with AI agents in a vendor-neutral way, and serves as the input to the IAADE control loop described in .

section.title: AI Agent Audit Log YANG Model
section.t: specifies in detail what an audit trail for an AI agent must contain: action timestamps, the anomaly that triggered the action, AI prompts and responses, pre- and post-action state snapshots, the outcome of the action, and whether the action was taken autonomously or required human approval.

This is a well-defined schema. Standardizing it as a YANG data model ensures that AI agent audit logs are interoperable across vendors and consumable by existing operational tooling. A minimum retention period of 365 days SHOULD be captured in the model as a configurable default.

section.title: AI Agent Configuration and Governance YANG Model
section.t: The governance parameters described in — the Action Registry, operator-managed Allow List and Block List, rate limit parameters (actions per hour, per-target limits, cooldown periods), protected resource targets, and degradation level thresholds — are all operator-configured state.

Standardizing these as a YANG data model allows operators to configure and audit AI agent governance through the same NETCONF/RESTCONF interfaces they already use, and enables consistent policy enforcement across heterogeneous deployments.

section.title: AI Agent Operational State YANG Model
section.t: The runtime state of an AI agent — its current degradation level, active rate-limit counters, retry counts per target, escalation state, and current execution mode (autonomous versus monitoring-only) — constitutes operational state that operators need to observe.

This model is further informed by the IAADE control loop defined in . The operational state SHOULD include the current phase of the IAADE loop (Intent, Awareness, Analysis, Decision, or Execution), the active intent being processed, and for each phase: the inputs consumed, the outputs produced, and any exceptions encountered. This gives operators visibility into not only the health of the AI agent but also what it is currently doing and why.

Standardizing this as a YANG operational state model allows operators to monitor AI agent health and activity through existing telemetry and streaming telemetry infrastructure, using the same tools they use to observe network device state.

section.title: NETCONF/RESTCONF Extensions for AI Agents
section.t: Three gaps in NETCONF and RESTCONF must be addressed to support AI-driven network management.

First, when an AI agent modifies device configuration via NETCONF or RESTCONF, there is currently no standard way to record that the change was AI-initiated in the datastore commit journal or in NETCONF notification streams. IETF should define a metadata attribute for NETCONF <edit-config> operations and RESTCONF PATCH requests that identifies the change as AI-initiated, carries the agent identity, and references the audit log entry that triggered the action. A corresponding extension to NETCONF notifications should carry this attribution so that downstream consumers such as network management systems and SIEM tools can correlate configuration events with AI agent activity.

Second, requires that the controller northbound interface support intent-based RPCs — operations that accept a high-level operational goal (e.g., "optimize-performance for service X") rather than specific configuration parameters, and return status on goal fulfillment. This is qualitatively different from the request-response model of existing NETCONF RPCs and requires new RPC definitions that can carry structured intents as inputs and fulfillment status as outputs.

Third, an NMA may run multiple concurrent management sessions for independent intents, each requiring strict context isolation to prevent state interference between parallel tasks. NETCONF's existing session model does not provide this isolation natively. IETF should define a lightweight context identifier mechanism for NETCONF sessions that allows a single transport connection to multiplex independent agent task contexts.
This work should be pursued in the NETCONF WG, coordinated with either OPSAWG orthe AINETOPS WG, depending on where the A2U interface work is chartered.

section.title: Agent-to-Controller Interface (NMOP)
section.t: identifies the Agent-to-Controller (A2C) interface — the east-west interface between an independently deployed NMA and an existing SDN controller — as a required component of the NMA architecture, but explicitly defers its definition to future documents.

This interface is a concrete NMOP WG work item. It should define how the NMA invokes controller capabilities (topology queries, telemetry subscriptions, configuration enforcement) as abstract tools, and how the controller returns results and exceptions to the NMA. The interface should be expressed using existing IETF management protocols (NETCONF/RESTCONF or gNMI) and YANG data models, avoiding the introduction of a new protocol.

section.title: Operational Security BCP
section.t: The security considerations specific to AI-mediated network management are qualitatively different from those of traditional management plane access. They include:

ul.li: The AI service as an attack surface: prompt injection via device-generated telemetry strings included in AI prompts, and compromise of the AI service API endpoint.
Credential management: AI agents require management plane credentials that must not appear in plaintext configuration or AI prompts.
Audit trail integrity: write-ahead logging with integrity protection and forwarding to external aggregators to prevent tampering.
Exfiltration risk: AI agents that send telemetry to external AI services may inadvertently exfiltrate topology, credential, or customer data.

section.t: A BCP document addressing these considerations would give operators deploying AI agents a normative security baseline independent of vendor implementation. This work is suitable for OPSAWG.

section.title: Governance Principles as Informational RFC
section.t: The thirteen governance principles defined in are well-developed and provide a sound foundation for implementors. Publishing this document as an Informational RFC would give it stable citable status without requiring it to carry normative weight. The normative content — the specific configurations and behaviors that implement these principles — is better expressed in the YANG data models described in .

section.title: Out of Scope
section.t: The following are explicitly out of scope for the proposed IETF work:

dl.dd: High-level architecture descriptions of how AI fits into network management are better suited to IRTF or IAB documents. IETF work should start from a defined architecture and produce data models and protocol extensions for it, not define the architecture itself.

Specifying which algorithms to use for anomaly detection, classification, or remediation recommendation is outside IETF scope. The YANG data models and protocol extensions proposed here are intentionally AI-service-agnostic.
The use cases and requirements document is sufficient to scope the work. Ongoing gap analysis documents without associated data model or protocol deliverables should not be chartered work items.
Guidelines for aligning IETF YANG data models with TMF, 3GPP, or other external frameworks are outside IETF scope and should be addressed through liaison statements rather than chartered work items.

section.title: Summary of Proposed Deliverables
section.t: The following table summarizes the proposed deliverables, their intended publication track, and suggested home working group.

tr.td: AI agent audit log YANG model
AI agent configuration and governance YANG model
AI agent operational state YANG model (incl. IAADE phases)
NETCONF session context isolation for concurrent agent tasks
Operational security BCP for AI-mediated management

section.title: Operational Considerations
section.t: Operators deploying AI agents for network management should ensure that the management interfaces used by AI agents are subject to the same access control, logging, and change management policies as human operator access. The YANG data models proposed in are designed to integrate with existing NETCONF/RESTCONF management infrastructure rather than requiring separate management planes for AI agents.

section.title: Security Considerations
section.t: The security considerations for AI-mediated network management are discussed in . Key concerns include prompt injection via device-generated data, credential exposure to AI services, audit trail integrity, and telemetry exfiltration. These are addressed in detail in .

section.title: IANA Considerations
section.t: This document has no IANA actions.

section.title: Acknowledgments
section.t: The recommendations in this document were informed by discussions on the OPSAWG and AINETOPS mailing lists, and by the presentations at IETF 125. The governance framework proposed in by Andy Smith and Nalin Pai provided the concrete foundation from which the YANG data model proposals are derived.
